reason: The paper does use RF's "built-in" (ranger, no permutation specified) importance scores to rank ASVs and this ranking is central: the headline finding is that "One ASV with an exact match to Rothia dentocariosa...was highly ranked as predictive across all four disparate sample types." However, unlike a case where the ranking rests only on in-sample impurity scores or overall held-out AUC, the authors independently replicate this specific top-ranked feature in external, non-CV data: they show the same Rothia ASV differentiates SARS-CoV-2 status in a separate pre-pandemic 2016 ICU cohort ("we found that high prevalence of this Rothia ASV is specific to SARS-CoV-2 positive patient samples") and further validate a related biological association (cardiovascular disease) in the independent American Gut Project dataset (Fisher's exact p=0.041). This constitutes genuine external, held-out validation of the selected top feature itself, not merely overall model accuracy, so the impurity-importance bias (which affects relative magnitude/ranking among correlated/skewed features within one RF run) is unlikely to have produced a spurious central conclusion. The secondary "top 40 genera" list (Actinomyces, Anaerococcus, etc.) is not independently validated and is only weakly supported by prior-literature plausibility, but it is not the paper's central conclusion, which is squarely the cross-validated, externally replicated Rothia finding.
